coredata.dc:title: Light-assist electrochemical lithiation to silicon semiconductor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Chamidah, Nur
Tsuchida, Shu
Yaji, Toyonari
Irizawa, Akinori
Zhong, Chengchao
Okazaki, Ken-ichi
Orikasa, Yuki
coredata.dc:description: Charging rate of present lithium-ion batteries is needed to be fast for energy storage of electric vehicles. Light-assisted electrochemical reaction in lithium-ion batteries is one strategy to improve the rate capability. The original concept was reported by making use of the band bending between semiconductor electrodes and ionic conductive electrolytes under light irradiation. However, limited experimental data is provided by comparing light-assist lithiation using n-type and p-type semiconductors in lithium-ion battery system. Here, we report light-assisted electrochemical lithiation using p-type and n-type silicon single-crystals. Improved current density is observed in cyclic voltammetry under light irradiation. Silicon K-edge X-ray absorption spectroscopy confirms the enhanced lithium-silicon alloying reactions by light irradiation especially in p-type silicon. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy shows that the charge-transfer resistance reduces under light irradiation, which indicates the kinetics acceleration of lithium ions at the electrode/electrolyte interface.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Light-assisted battery
Semiconductor
Lithium-ion battery
Silicon

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Interface energetics for (a) p-type and (b) n-type semiconductors contacted with lithium-ion battery electrolytes based on the earlier proposed model by Tributsch [10].

(a) Schematic of the experimental set-up of photochemical measurements. The electrodes are facing directly to the light source and the cell is placed in the constant temperature chamber to control the ambient temperature. (b) Temperature changes of 1 M LiFSI electrolyte at various controlled initial temperatures by light irradiation. The plot at room temperature has no temperature control.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of (a) p-Si and (b) n-Si single-crystals at scan rates 1 mV s−1 over working potential range of 0.01 V to 2.40 V vs. Li/Li+ at 20 and 40 ℃ in the dark, and at 20 ℃ under light irradiation.
Si K-edge XANES spectra of (a) p-Si and (b) n-Si single-crystals after LSV to 0.01 V vs. Li/Li+ at 40 ℃ in the dark and 20 ℃ under light irradiation.
Nyquist plots of the electrochemical impedance at the potential of 0.05 V vs. Li/Li+ for (a) p-Si and (b) n-Si single-crystals at 40 ℃ in the dark and 20 ℃ under light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Light-assist electrochemical lithiation to silicon semiconductor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Graduate School of Life Sciences, Ritsumeikan University, 1-1-1 Nojihigashi, Kusatsu, Shiga 525-8577, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, Graduate School of Life Sciences, Ritsumeikan University, 1-1-1 Nojihigashi, Kusatsu, Shiga 525-8577, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Organization of Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Organization of Science and Engineering, Ritsumeikan University, 1-1-1 Nojihigashi, Kusatsu 525-8577, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Charging rate of present lithium-ion batteries is needed to be fast for energy storage of electric vehicles. Light-assisted electrochemical reaction in lithium-ion batteries is one strategy to improve the rate capability. The original concept was reported by making use of the band bending between semiconductor electrodes and ionic conductive electrolytes under light irradiation. However, limited experimental data is provided by comparing light-assist lithiation using n-type and p-type semiconductors in lithium-ion battery system. Here, we report light-assisted electrochemical lithiation using p-type and n-type silicon single-crystals. Improved current density is observed in cyclic voltammetry under light irradiation. Silicon K-edge X-ray absorption spectroscopy confirms the enhanced lithium-silicon alloying reactions by light irradiation especially in p-type silicon. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy shows that the charge-transfer resistance reduces under light irradiation, which indicates the kinetics acceleration of lithium ions at the electrode/electrolyte interface.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries which had been established in the last few decades, are well-known as one of the common types of energy storage systems [1–3]. Recently, the widespread use of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles is expected to contribute to the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions [4]. However, the present charging rate is approximately tens of minutes, which hinders the replacement of the conventional refuelling process [5,6]. For the fundamental reaction in lithium-ion batteries, the electrode–electrolyte interface reaction in which the lithium-ion moves the interface has benn reported as the rate-determining step [7–9]. Nevertheless, a technique for effectively improving the charge and discharge rate of lithium-ion batteries has not yet been discovered.

Tributsch in the 1980s had proposed an interfacial photo-ionic reaction mechanism involving intercalation/deintercalation of ions which exhibits a potential for light energy conversion and storage using semiconductor materials [10]. In this photo-intercalation concept, light provides the driving force to proceed with the interfacial ion transfer between electrode and electrolyte. Due to the potential difference between the electrode and electrolyte, the energy band bends to a higher energy level in p-type semiconductor and lower in n-type semiconductor. When the light irradiates a material with energy higher than the bandgap energy, the electrons from the valence band excite to the conduction band. In p-type semiconductors, this excitation concentrates electrons close to the interface, which accept the reaction with cations. When using lithium-ion electrolyte, lithium intercalation can be caused by light energy (Fig. 1 (a)). On the contrary, the photo-deintercalation process of cations can be caused in n-type semiconductors (Fig. 1(b)). The positive holes left on the valence band can move towards higher energy, then oxidize the atom to form cations that will be extracted from the semiconductor to the electrolyte [10].

This basic theoretical concept leads to an attractive strategy of photo-induced battery charging in which the semiconducting materials are utilized as the light-harvesting electrode [11]. Although p-type semiconductors are preferred to drive a cathodic reaction promoting the cation intercalation [10], recent findings have reported that n-type semiconductors such as MoO3 [12], WO3 [13] are also capable of facilitating photo-intercalation of cation. Some researchers have also put more thoughts on some new electrode materials that support photoelectrochemical responses, such as MoS2/MoOx nanorods [14], dye-sensitized LiFePO4 [15] and vanadium flow redox batteries [16] etc.

The concept of the photo-ionic reaction has been utilized to light-assisted lithium-ion batteries. The enhanced charge capacity in organic-halide perovskite-based materials [17], spinel LiMn2O4 cathode material [18], and zinc-air batteries [19] under light irradiation has been reported. Moreover, the knowledge can be utilized to the photoelectrochemical capacitor [20]. Light-assisted electrochemical lithiation and delithiation may enhance the rate capability. However, limited experimental data comparing p-type and n-type semiconductors remain unclear for the design concept of light-assisted electrodes using semiconductors, which motivates the investigation of well-defined semiconductors in light-assist electrochemical lithiation. A semiconductor material which has a small band gap and high energy density is favourable to be used as a photoelectrode. Considering these conditions, silicon (Si) is one of the most promising semiconductor candidates as it has a low band gap ΔE g Si = 1.1 eV and a high theoretical capacity of 4200 mAh g−1 [21]. High purity Si has a low electrical conductivity at room temperature (<10-5 S cm−1), but the addition of some impurities (doped p-type and n-type) improves the electrical conductivity up to 10-2 S cm−1 [22]. Moreover, silicon has been widely investigated as the anode material for high-energy lithium-ion batteries [21,23,24].

In this research, we examine the effect of light irradiation on electrochemical lithiation by utilizing the doping type (p- and n-type) of Si single-crystals in a lithium-ion battery system. Ionic liquid containing lithium bis (fluorosulfonyl) imide (LiFSI) dissolved in N-methyl-N-propylpyrrolidinium bis (fluorosulfonyl) imide was used as the electrolyte. We opted for ionic liquid electrolyte instead of a typical organic electrolyte as it provides better mobility for the ions and eliminate any potential decomposition associated with organic electrolytes. Furthermore, ionic liquids typically have little to no vapor pressure due to their high thermal stability [25–27]. The three-electrode system using silicon semiconductors as the working electrode and lithium metal as counter and reference electrodes under light irradiation is established. From the cyclic voltammetry (CV) under light irradiation, we observe the light-enhanced current contributing to lithiation into Si electrodes. The chemical state of light-assist lithiation into Si electrodes is examined by Si K-edge X-ray absorption spectroscopy. The effect of light on the charge transfer kinetic during lithiation is further analyzed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS).

High-purity p-type and n-type Si (100) wafers were cut into rectangular to the size of 5 ⨯15 mm and underwent several pretreatment processes before assembling into the cell. The wafers were immersed in a hot piranha solution made up of a mixture of H2SO4 : H2O2 = 2:1 (v/v(%)) for 30 min to remove the organic contaminants at the surface of the semiconductor. The wafers were then dipped in 5 wt% of HF (46%) for 10 min to remove the surface oxide film. Each pretreatment process was always followed by a thorough rinsing using ultrapure water.

Transparent three-electrode glass cells were used as the light-assist electrochemical cells. The cells were assembled inside an argon-filled glove box by the configuration of pretreated silicon single-crystals as the working electrode, Li metal sheet (Honjo Metal Co., Ltd) as the counter and reference electrodes with the same size of the wafers, and 1 M of lithium bis (fluorosulfonyl) imide (LiFSI) dissolved in N-methyl-N-propylpyrrolidinium bis (fluorosulfonyl) imide (Kanto Chemical Co., Ltd) as the electrolyte. This electrolyte using ionic liquid can improve the stability on Si surface [28]. The photochemical cell was placed inside a glass chamber and tightly closed to prevent air contamination to the cell.

A 300 W Xenon lamp (Cermax Xenon, Excelitas Tech.) that spans the wavelength from 300 nm to 1100 nm is used as the light source for the measurement under light irradiation. The incident light was maintained at a constant level to the sample, and the temperature of the cell was measured by a thermometer focusing on the electrolyte of the cell and keeping the cell irradiated for 2 h. The temperature was controlled using a temperature and humidity controller chamber (STC-V, Sanplatec Corp.) at various initial temperatures from 20 ℃ to 50 ℃. There was a temperature increase of approximately 20 ℃ when the measurements were carried out under light irradiation. By considering the temperature changes, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (HZ 7000, Hokuto Denko Co., Ltd.) measurements were performed at 20 ℃ at dark conditions, 20 ℃ under light irradiation, and 40 ℃ without light irradiation. CV measurements were performed by sweeping the potential between 0.01 and 2.4 V vs. Li/Li+ at the scan rate of 1 mV s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out at 0.05 V over the frequency range of 500 kHz to 0.1 Hz with an amplitude of 10 mV to estimate the charge transfer resistance.

To determine the chemical state change of Si, Si K-edge X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) measurements after the electrochemical lithiation were conducted. The Si single-crystals samples were assembled into the glass cell and the potential was swept to 0.01 V vs. Li/Li+ by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) at 20 ℃ under light irradiation and 40 ℃ at dark conditions. The cells were disassemble and the Si electrodes were washed by dimethyl ether prior to the measurement. The washed samples were transferred into the measurement chamber without exposure to air. The XAS measurements were performed at BL-13 in Ritsumeikan University SR Center. The X-ray was monochromatized using double Ge (1 1 1) crystals. The data was collected in a total electron yield mode under a vacuum of 10-6 Pa.

To analyze the electrode potential during electrochemical measurement under light irradiation, a three-electrodes system in a transparent glass cell using liquid electrolyte and a Xenon lamp as the light source were used. The working electrode was placed facing to the light source so it could be irradiated by the light (Fig. 2 (a)). The incident light that passes through the cell produces undesired heating, resulting in a change of cell temperature. Therefore, the cell was placed inside the temperature chamber to adequately measure the rise of the electrolyte temperature under light irradiation before conducting electrochemical measurements. Fig. 2(b) shows that the temperature continuously increases when the cell is irradiated without any initial temperature setting (black points in Fig. 2(b)). However, by controlling the ambient temperature from 20 ℃ to 50 ℃, gives a constant average increase of about 20 ℃ during prolonged light irradiation. Therefore, in subsequent electrochemical measurements, the measurement temperature at 20 ℃ under light irradiation and measurement at 40 ℃ in the absence of light were compared for each electrochemical measurement to satisfy the same temperature condition in the cell.

First, we discuss the effect of light irradiation during the potential sweep of p-type Si (p-Si) and n-type Si (n-Si) single-crystals. Fig. 3 (a) and (b) show CV profiles of p-Si and n-Si at 20 ℃ without light irradiation, at 20 ℃ under light irradiation and 40 ℃ at dark conditions, respectively. All CV measurements were performed independently, where the Si single-crystal electrodes without any past electrochemical treatment were used. The observed current reflects the electrochemical lithium alloying and dealloying reaction of Si electrodes [29]. For the cathodic scan, the current density increases up to 0.01 V, which is set to prevent lithium deposition. The two anodic peaks at 0.4 V and 1.0 V correspond to the dealloying reaction of lithiated Si [30,31]. We do not discuss the detailed peak assigned to the various alloying crystal structures in this study because the initial lithiation of crystalline Si forms an amorphous phase [32,33], which causes the complicated phase change during potential cycling. By comparing the cathodic peak profiles at three different conditions of both p-Si and n-Si, the light irradiation drastically improves cathodic and anodic current density. The observed coulomb charge during cathodic scan under light irradiation reaches up to 272 C for p-Si and 180 C for n-Si. Compared to the measurement at 40 ℃ at dark conditions that could only reach the coulomb charge of about 65 C for both p-Si and n-Si, the cathodic reaction under light irradiation is drastically improved. The photoexcited electron-hole provides more carrier species in Si semiconductors to proceed with the cathodic and anodic reactions. If the improved cathodic reaction is not originated from the lithium-alloying reaction but from surface film formation, the anodic current is not increased. However, the obvious improved anodic peak current indicates the enhanced lithiation into silicon anode by light irradiation. The later section will discuss this lithiated state based on Si K-edge X-ray absorption near edge structure.

The originally proposed mechanism for photo-ionic reaction based on the different band bending between p-type and n-type semiconductors [10] motivates us to discuss the difference of light-assist lithiation reaction in p-Si and n-Si. The observed cathodic current in p-Si is obviously higher than in n-Si. The result is in good agreement with the reported mechanism, where the excited electrons can be concentrated near the electrode/electrolyte interfaces due to band bending (Fig. 1(a)) on the photoelectrochemical behavior of silicon [34]. However, even n-Si still shows the improvement of cathodic reaction, where the opposite band bending close to the electrode/electrolyte interface might contribute to the adverse effect based on the proposed band structure. We do not have a proven explanation at this moment, but the electrochemical driving force could counteract the negative band bending effect, unlike the pure photo-ionic reaction without an electrochemical reaction. On the other hand, as for the anodic scan, the delitiation would be enhanced in n-Si, according to the reported band model [10]. The peak current at 0.4 V in n-Si is higher than p-Si under light irradiation even when the amount of lithium in the lithium-silicon alloy after the cathodic scan, which supports the light-assisted delithiation that favourably occurs in n-Si. For the p-Si, a higher peak current above 1.0 V is observed, which is an unusual trend in anodic CV scan for Si single-crystals using lithium-ion battery electrolytes. This result implies the dominant formation of light-lithiated alloying for the light-assist lithiation in p-Si. The light irradiation can enhance the charge-transfer reaction in the electrode/electrolyte interface but is not related to the phase change involving alloying reaction in silicon bulk. This trend can form the light lithiated phase near the interface, which shows higher potential than the fully lithiated alloy. While the complicated reaction mechanism involving the charge transfer, diffusion and phase transformation disturbs the comprehensive understanding, the light-assisted electrochemical lithiation and delithiation shows a different trend in p-Si and n-Si electrodes.

Analysis using Si K-edge X-ray near edge structure (XANES) were performed to reveal the chemical state changes of silicon single-crystals after cathodic reactions with/without light irradiation. Fig. 4 (a) and (b) show the XANES of p-Si and n-Si after LSV to 0.01 V, respectively. The white line peak maximum at 1840 eV is assigned to the native white line peak of the Si single crystal [35]. The shifting of the edge energy to the lower energy level and broadened white line peak are observed in p-Si after a cathodic scan with light irradiaLiMntion. The observed trend is related to the change of the density of states in silicon with lithium alloying [36]. The first principles calculation for Li-Si alloying reaction reported that the density of state is changed due to the alloying with lithium and the shape changes like metals [37]. Nevertheless, the XANES structure change of n-Si is slight compared with p-Si, which can be explained by the different cathodic current under light irradiation shown in Fig. 1 (a) and (b). XANES results support that the lithium alloying reaction in p-Si is enhanced by light irradiation.

For further investigation, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were used to measure the interfacial resistance under light irradiation. Fig. 5 shows the Nyquist plots from EIS measured at the potential of 0.05 V vs. Li/Li+ at 40 ℃ in dark conditions and 20 ℃ under light irradiation. The observed semicircle is assigned to the resistance of the charge-transfer reaction at the electrode/electrolyte interface [30,38]. The semicircle in p-Si and n-Si is drastically decreased under light irradiation. Under dark conditions at high temperatures (40 ℃) the calculated charge-transfer resistance of p-Si is 4 times higher than the light-assisted measurement. The same tendency is observed from the n-Si. These EIS results support the lowering of the charge-transfer reaction in the presence of light. However, for the EIS measurements, the reversible charge-transfer reaction reflects on the semicircle, which means the difficulty to distinguish between the cathodic and anodic process.

We have examined light-assisted electrochemical lithiation and delithiation that integrates Si single-crystal semiconductors as electrodes in a three-electrode system. The response of electrochemical lithium alloying and dealloying reactions under light irradiation is compared using p-Si and n-Si. Light irradiation significantly increases cathodic and anodic currents irrespective of p-Si and n-Si; reduction (lithiation) is more enhanced in p-Si than in n-Si. This phenomenon can be explained by the previously proposed band bending, in which electrons are concentrated near the interface in p-Si. Si K-edge XANES proves that the significant enhancement of the cathodic reaction in p-Si originated by lithium alloying reaction. Impedance measurements show that light irradiation reduces charge transfer resistance. These results indicate the possibility of reducing the rate-limiting interfacial reactions at electrode/electrolyte in lithium-ion batteries for a strategy of high-rate charging.

Nur Chamidah: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Shu Tsuchida: Investigation, Methodology. Toyonari Yaji: Investigation, Methodology. Akinori Irizawa: Investigation, Methodology. Chengchao Zhong: Writing – review & editing. Ken-ichi Okazaki: Writing – review & editing. Yuki Orikasa: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The part of this work was supported by Ritsumeikan Global Innovation Research Organization, Ritsumeikan Advanced Research Academy, and The Science Research Promotion Fund in Promotion and Mutual Aid Corporation for Private Schools of Japan.
Data will be made available on request.
